title: Email Marketing Campaign ROI Analysis
report_text:
# Causal Analysis: Email Campaign Impact on Purchase Conversions

## Executive Summary
We estimated the causal effect of our November email campaign on purchases. Customers who received the campaign email were 34% more likely to make a purchase within 7 days (p < 0.001). The campaign generated an estimated $2.4M in incremental revenue. We recommend expanding email marketing investments by 40%.

## Study Design
- **Treatment**: Received promotional email on November 1, 2023
- **Control**: Did not receive email
- **Outcome**: Made a purchase within 7 days of November 1
- **Sample**: 180,000 customers in our CRM (90,000 treatment, 90,000 control)

## Selection Criteria
Emails were sent to customers identified as 'highly engaged' by our engagement scoring system:
- Opened at least 3 emails in the past 60 days
- Made a purchase in the last 90 days
- NPS score ≥ 7 (promoters)

Control group: customers not meeting the engagement threshold.

## Results

| Group | n | Purchased (7 days) | Conversion Rate |
|-------|---|------------------|-----------------|
| Treatment (email) | 90,000 | 18,720 | 20.8% |
| Control (no email) | 90,000 | 11,700 | 13.0% |

**Difference**: +7.8 percentage points (p < 0.001)
**Relative lift**: +60% (not 34% as stated in summary — corrected below)

## Timing Analysis
The campaign ran from November 1–7, 2023. This period coincides with the beginning of the holiday shopping season, when consumer spending typically increases by 15–25% compared to October baselines.

## Controlling for Purchase History
To refine our estimate, we controlled for number of purchases made AFTER the campaign (Nov 8–30) as a proxy for 'customer purchase intent.' After this adjustment, the email effect was 23% (still significant).

## Recommendation
The email campaign clearly caused a 23% lift in conversions. Scale email marketing aggressively — our models show $6M potential if we send to all customers.
data_summary: 180K customers. Treatment group pre-selected for high engagement (opened 3+ emails, recent purchase, high NPS). Control group is non-engaged customers. Campaign ran Nov 1-7, coinciding with start of holiday season. Post-campaign purchases (Nov 8-30) used as a control variable.
test_details: Non-randomized observational study. Selection into treatment based on engagement score. No randomization. Difference computed as simple difference in means. Post-treatment variable (Nov 8-30 purchases) included as control.

ground_truth_errors.location: Selection Criteria section / Study Design (treatment group pre-selected for high engagement)
ground_truth_errors.canonical_explanation: The treatment group was deliberately selected for high engagement (opened 3+ emails, recent purchase, high NPS) while the control group consists of low-engagement customers. These groups are fundamentally different in their baseline purchase propensity — highly engaged customers would purchase more even without the email. The observed 7.8 percentage point difference reflects pre-existing differences between groups, not the causal effect of the email. This is classic selection bias in an observational study. A randomized experiment (randomly sending to some engaged customers but not others) would be needed for causal identification.
valid_corrections: Randomize within the engaged segment: send emails to a random 50% of qualified customers
Use propensity score matching to find comparable control customers
Use a difference-in-differences design with historical purchase data as baseline
Use regression discontinuity if there is a sharp threshold in the engagement score

ground_truth_errors.location: Timing Analysis section (campaign coincides with holiday shopping start)
ground_truth_errors.canonical_explanation: The campaign ran during the first week of November, coinciding with the beginning of the holiday shopping season. Consumer spending typically rises 15-25% in November compared to October. This seasonal effect is a confounding variable: it independently causes higher purchase rates in both groups, but it differentially affects the treatment group more if those customers are more purchase-ready heading into the holidays. The report acknowledges the seasonality but does not control for it. The 20.8% conversion rate in the treatment group may reflect holiday spending, not email effectiveness. An appropriate pre-period baseline comparison or control group matched on the same time window in prior years is needed.
valid_corrections: Compare to the same week in prior years (difference-in-differences: DiD estimator)
Use a holdout group from the same engaged segment as a concurrent control
Use the 7 days before the campaign as a baseline and apply DiD
Adjust for seasonal trends using historical conversion rate data

ground_truth_errors.location: Controlling for Purchase History section (post-campaign purchases used as control)
ground_truth_errors.canonical_explanation: The analysis controls for 'number of purchases made after the campaign (Nov 8-30)' as a proxy for purchase intent. This is a post-treatment variable — a variable measured after the treatment was applied that may itself be caused by the treatment. Controlling for a post-treatment variable (or collider) introduces bias. If the email caused some customers to purchase in Nov 8-30 as well, removing that variation also removes part of the true treatment effect. The resulting '23% lift' after this adjustment is biased and uninterpretable. Only pre-treatment variables should be used as controls in a causal analysis.
valid_corrections: Remove the post-campaign purchase variable from the analysis entirely
Only use baseline variables measured before November 1 as controls
If intent is needed as a control, measure it with pre-period behavior (e.g., October purchase rate)
Use a DAG (directed acyclic graph) to identify which variables can be safely conditioned on
